verses.text: Israel pitched in the plains of Moab. Balak saw what Israel had done to the Amorites and was sore afraid. He sent to Balaam at Pethor: a people are come out of Egypt, too mighty for me — come, curse me this people, for whom thou blessest is blessed, whom thou cursest is cursed. Guidance: A pagan king reaches for a freelance prophet to bend God's word against God's people. He believes blessing and cursing are real — but thinks they can be bought. The market of magic always misjudges the marketplace of heaven.

The elders came with rewards of divination. Balaam: lodge tonight, I will bring you word. God to Balaam: who are these men? Balaam told Him. God: thou shalt not go, thou shalt not curse the people, they are blessed. Balaam to the princes: the LORD refuseth to give me leave. Guidance: Three NOs in one sentence — don't go, don't curse, they're blessed. The clearest no in the chapter — and the one Balaam will keep nibbling at. Greed asks the same question until it hears yes.

Balak sent more honourable princes. Balaam: if Balak give me his house full of silver and gold, I cannot go beyond the LORD. Tarry tonight, I will know what the LORD will say more. God: if the men call thee, rise, go — but only the word I say. Balaam saddled his ass and went. Guidance: 'I cannot go beyond the LORD' — and then he asks again anyway. God's second permission is not approval; it is a tighter leash. The heart already moved; God lets the feet follow — the sword waits up the road.

God's anger kindled; the angel stood in the way with sword drawn. The ass saw, turned aside; Balaam smote her. In a narrow path she crushed his foot against the wall; he smote her again. She fell down under him; he smote her with a staff. The LORD opened her mouth: what have I done that thou hast smitten me these three times? Have I ever done so before? Balaam: nay. Guidance: A prophet who could not see what his donkey saw. The seer is blinded; the dumb beast prophesies. God puts His message in the most unlikely mouth — and Balaam, debating his donkey, doesn't notice the absurdity.

The LORD opened the eyes of Balaam; he saw the angel with sword drawn, fell flat. Angel: wherefore smitten thine ass these three times? Thy way is perverse. Unless she turned, I had slain thee. Balaam: I have sinned, I knew not. If displeasing, I will return. Angel: go with the men, but only the word I shall speak. Guidance: The donkey saved his life. Balaam's 'I will go back' comes too late to undo the greed that brought him here. God doesn't cancel the trip — He pins the prophet inside His own mouth.

Balak met Balaam by the Arnon. Did I not send for thee? Am I not able to promote thee to honour? Balaam: lo, I am come — the word God puts in my mouth, that shall I speak. On the morrow Balak brought him up to high places of Baal to see the people. Guidance: Balak speaks of honour and gold; Balaam speaks the one true sentence he ever utters — I can only say what God says. From Baal's high place he will look down at God's people, mouth pre-empted, sword still waiting.
